weeks.title: Week 1 — Vite setup + React core concepts
weeks.description: Understand how React thinks. Components, JSX, props, and state are everything.

days.label: Day 1 · Monday
days.title: Scaffold your project with Vite

tasks.text: Run: npm create vite@latest my-shop -- --template react
tasks.description: Vite is the modern, fast build tool for React — the standard choice for new projects.

tasks.text: Run: npm install, then npm run dev — confirm app opens in browser
tasks.description: Confirming the dev server runs proves your toolchain (Node, npm, Vite) is set up correctly.

tasks.text: Explore the folder structure: src/, public/, index.html, vite.config.js
tasks.description: Knowing where files live (src/, public/, config) is essential before you start adding your own code.

tasks.text: Understand: what index.html does, what main.jsx does, what App.jsx does
tasks.description: These three files are the entry point of every React app — index.html loads main.jsx, which mounts App.jsx.

tasks.text: Install Tailwind CSS: follow tailwindcss.com/docs/guides/vite exactly
tasks.description: Tailwind lets you style components with utility classes directly in JSX — no separate CSS files to manage.

tasks.text: Confirm Tailwind works: add className="text-blue-500" to a heading and check color
tasks.description: Confirming a utility class renders correctly verifies Tailwind is properly configured before you build anything.

days.resource: Resource: vitejs.dev for Vite docs · tailwindcss.com/docs for Tailwind installation

days.label: Day 2 · Tuesday
days.title: JSX and components

tasks.text: Learn: JSX is not HTML — className not class, htmlFor not for, self-closing tags
tasks.description: JSX looks like HTML but compiles to JavaScript — these naming differences trip up every beginner at first.

tasks.text: Learn: a component is just a function that returns JSX
tasks.description: Understanding components as functions demystifies React — there's no magic, just functions returning JSX.

tasks.text: Learn: component names must start with uppercase (Button not button)
tasks.description: React treats lowercase tags as HTML elements and uppercase as components — this naming rule is how React tells them apart.

tasks.text: Build: Button, Card, Badge components in a src/components/ folder
tasks.description: Building small reusable components is the core React skill — every UI is composed from pieces like these.

tasks.text: Use them in App.jsx — compose them together like HTML elements
tasks.description: Composing components together is how complex UIs are built from simple building blocks.

tasks.text: Style with Tailwind utility classes — no separate CSS file needed
tasks.description: Styling with Tailwind directly in components keeps styles colocated with the markup they affect.

days.resource: Resource: react.dev/learn — the new official React docs. Far better than any course. Start at "Your First Component".

tasks.text: Learn: props are how a parent passes data down to a child component
tasks.description: Props are how data flows down the component tree — the foundation of React's one-way data flow.

tasks.text: Learn: destructure props in the function signature — ({title, price}) not (props)
tasks.description: Destructuring props makes components easier to read and shows exactly what data they expect.

tasks.text: Learn: the children prop — what renders between opening and closing component tags
tasks.description: The children prop lets components wrap arbitrary content — used heavily by layout and modal components.

tasks.text: Build: ProductCard component that receives name, price, imageUrl, category as props
tasks.description: ProductCard is the central component of your storefront — every product listing will reuse it.

tasks.text: Render a static list of 3 ProductCards in App.jsx with different props each
tasks.description: Rendering multiple cards with different props proves the component is properly reusable.

days.label: Day 4 · Thursday
days.title: State with useState

tasks.text: Learn: what state is — data that changes, and when it changes the UI re-renders
tasks.description: Understanding the render cycle is essential — React re-renders whenever state changes, and that's how UI updates happen.

tasks.text: Learn: const [value, setValue] = useState(initialValue)
tasks.description: useState is the most-used hook in React — nearly every interactive component needs it.

tasks.text: Learn: never mutate state directly — always call the setter function
tasks.description: Mutating state directly doesn't trigger a re-render and can cause subtle bugs — always use the setter.

tasks.text: Build: a counter component with increment/decrement buttons
tasks.description: A counter is the classic first useState exercise — small enough to focus purely on the state update pattern.

tasks.text: Build: a controlled text input — state mirrors input value in real-time
tasks.description: Controlled inputs are the standard way to handle form fields in React — you'll use this pattern constantly.

tasks.text: Build: a simple toggle (show/hide a div) using boolean state
tasks.description: A toggle is a simple but common UI pattern — modals, dropdowns, and accordions all build on this.

days.resource: Mental model: Every time you call a setter, React re-runs your component function and redraws the UI. State is the memory between renders.

tasks.text: Learn: render a list with .map() — every item needs a unique key prop
tasks.description: .map() with keys is how every list of data (products, comments, orders) gets rendered in React.

tasks.text: Learn: conditional rendering — {condition && } and ternary operator
tasks.description: Conditional rendering is how UI responds to state — showing/hiding elements based on data or user actions.

tasks.text: Learn: event handlers — onClick, onChange, onSubmit — always camelCase
tasks.description: Event handlers are how your components respond to user interaction — clicks, typing, submissions.

tasks.text: Build: product list rendered with .map() from an array of objects
tasks.description: Rendering a product list from data is the exact pattern your storefront's product grid will use.

tasks.text: Build: a search input that filters the displayed products in real time
tasks.description: A live search filter combines state, events, and list rendering — a realistic feature you'll see in real apps.

tasks.text: Push project to GitHub — commit after every feature you build
tasks.description: Committing after each feature creates a clean history that's easy to review and roll back if needed.

weeks.askPrompt: Explain React state (useState) to me like I am a complete beginner — with a simple analogy and a short practical code example

weeks.title: Week 2 — useEffect + React Router v6
weeks.description: Add side effects and multi-page navigation. Your app starts feeling real.

days.label: Day 1 · Monday
days.title: useEffect — side effects in React

tasks.text: Learn: useEffect runs after render — for fetching, subscriptions, timers
tasks.description: useEffect is how React handles anything outside of rendering — fetching data, timers, subscriptions.

tasks.text: Learn: the dependency array — [] runs once, [value] runs when value changes
tasks.description: The dependency array controls when an effect re-runs — getting it wrong is one of the most common React bugs.

tasks.text: Learn: cleanup function — returned from useEffect, runs before next effect or unmount
tasks.description: Cleanup functions prevent memory leaks and stale subscriptions when a component unmounts or re-runs.

tasks.text: Build: fetch products from jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts inside useEffect
tasks.description: Fetching data with useEffect is the manual approach you'll later replace with TanStack Query — understanding it first makes the upgrade meaningful.

tasks.text: Build: loading state (show spinner) and error state (show error message)
tasks.description: Loading and error states are required for any real data-fetching UI — users need feedback while waiting or when something fails.

days.resource: Note: In Week 3 you'll replace useEffect fetching with TanStack Query — which handles loading/error/caching far better. This week is just to understand the underlying mechanism.

tasks.text: Install: npm install react-router-dom
tasks.description: react-router-dom is the standard routing library for React — required for any multi-page app.

tasks.text: Learn: wrap app in BrowserRouter in main.jsx
tasks.description: BrowserRouter enables client-side routing — navigating without full page reloads.

tasks.text: Learn: Routes and Route components — path="/products" element={<ProductsPage />}
tasks.description: Routes and Route map URL paths to the components that should render for them.

tasks.text: Learn: Link component — replaces <a> tags for client-side navigation (no page reload)
tasks.description: Link enables instant navigation without a full page reload — a core benefit of single-page apps.

tasks.text: Build: pages folder — HomePage, ProductsPage, AboutPage
tasks.description: Organizing pages into a folder mirrors how real React apps structure their routes.

tasks.text: Build: Navbar with Link components to each page
tasks.description: A Navbar with links is how users move between the different sections of your app.

tasks.text: Learn: dynamic route — path="/products/:id"
tasks.description: Dynamic routes let one route definition handle many URLs — e.g. one page for every product ID.

tasks.text: Learn: useParams() hook — extract :id from the URL inside a component
tasks.description: useParams() reads the dynamic segment from the URL so your component knows which item to display.

tasks.text: Learn: useNavigate() hook — programmatic navigation (e.g. after form submit)
tasks.description: useNavigate() lets you redirect users programmatically — e.g. after a successful form submission.

tasks.text: Build: ProductDetailPage — reads id from URL, fetches that product, displays it
tasks.description: ProductDetailPage demonstrates the full pattern: read the URL, fetch the matching data, render it.

tasks.text: Make each ProductCard's title clickable — navigate to /products/:id
tasks.description: Making cards clickable connects your product list to the detail page — a core navigation flow.

days.label: Day 4 · Thursday
days.title: Nested routes + layouts

tasks.text: Learn: nested Route — child routes share parent layout
tasks.description: Nested routes let multiple pages share a common layout (like Navbar/Footer) without duplicating code.

tasks.text: Learn: Outlet component — where child routes render inside a layout
tasks.description: Outlet is where React Router renders the matched child route inside a parent layout.

tasks.text: Build: RootLayout with Navbar + Footer — wraps all pages via Outlet
tasks.description: A RootLayout with Outlet avoids repeating the Navbar and Footer on every page.

tasks.text: Build: a 404 NotFoundPage — render it with path="*"
tasks.description: A 404 page handles any URL that doesn't match a defined route — a polish detail every real app needs.

tasks.text: Learn: NavLink vs Link — NavLink adds active class styling automatically
tasks.description: NavLink's automatic active styling makes it easy to highlight the current page in navigation.

days.resource: Resource: reactrouter.com/en/main/start/tutorial — official React Router tutorial, very well written

tasks.text: Learn: controlled input — value tied to state, onChange updates state
tasks.description: Controlled inputs keep form state in sync with React state — the standard way to read and validate input.

tasks.text: Learn: form onSubmit — always call e.preventDefault() first
tasks.description: preventDefault() stops the browser's default full-page form submission so React can handle it instead.

tasks.text: Learn: managing multiple form fields with one object state
tasks.description: A single object for form state avoids one useState per field and keeps form logic manageable.

tasks.text: Build: a contact form — name, email, message fields with validation feedback
tasks.description: A contact form with validation feedback is a realistic form pattern you'll reuse for login, register, and product forms.

tasks.text: Build: client-side validation — show error messages if fields are empty on submit
tasks.description: Client-side validation gives users immediate feedback before a request is even sent to the server.

weeks.askPrompt: Show me how to set up React Router v6 with nested routes and a shared layout component using Outlet — with a complete working code example

weeks.title: Week 3 — TanStack Query (React Query)
weeks.description: Replace all your manual fetch + useEffect code with the industry-standard data fetching library.

days.label: Day 1 · Monday
days.title: Why TanStack Query and setup

tasks.text: Install: npm install @tanstack/react-query @tanstack/react-query-devtools
tasks.description: TanStack Query is the industry-standard library for fetching, caching, and syncing server data in React.

tasks.text: Learn: what TanStack Query solves — caching, background refetch, loading/error states, deduplication
tasks.description: Understanding what problems it solves explains why it's preferred over manual useEffect fetching.

tasks.text: Wrap app in QueryClientProvider with a new QueryClient() in main.jsx
tasks.description: QueryClientProvider makes the query cache available to every component in your app.

tasks.text: Add ReactQueryDevtools to App.jsx — open the devtools panel in browser
tasks.description: The devtools panel lets you visually inspect cached queries, their status, and refetch behavior.

tasks.text: Create src/api/products.js — a plain fetch function that returns products array
tasks.description: A plain fetch function separates the 'how to get data' logic from the 'how to use data' logic in components.

days.resource: Resource: tanstack.com/query/latest/docs — "Quick Start" and "Queries" sections. This is the best docs page in the React ecosystem.

tasks.text: Learn: useQuery({ queryKey: ['products'], queryFn: fetchProducts })
tasks.description: useQuery is the core hook for fetching and caching data — replaces useEffect + fetch + manual state.

tasks.text: Learn: destructure { data, isLoading, isError, error } from useQuery
tasks.description: These returned values give you everything needed to handle loading, error, and success states declaratively.

tasks.text: Learn: queryKey is the cache key — ['products', id] for a single product
tasks.description: The queryKey uniquely identifies cached data — including variables like id keeps each item cached separately.

tasks.text: Rewrite ProductsPage to use useQuery instead of useEffect + fetch
tasks.description: Rewriting ProductsPage with useQuery removes manual loading/error state management entirely.

tasks.text: Rewrite ProductDetailPage to use useQuery(['products', id], fetchProductById)
tasks.description: A parameterized query key lets TanStack Query cache each product detail page separately.

tasks.text: Watch devtools — navigate between pages, observe cache hits (no refetch)
tasks.description: Watching cache hits in devtools shows the performance benefit of TanStack Query's caching firsthand.

days.label: Day 3 · Wednesday
days.title: useMutation — create, update, delete

tasks.text: Learn: useMutation for POST/PUT/DELETE — different from useQuery (not automatic)
tasks.description: useMutation handles one-off operations like creating or deleting data, unlike useQuery's automatic refetching.

tasks.text: Learn: mutate() and mutateAsync() — how to trigger the mutation
tasks.description: mutate() and mutateAsync() are how you trigger a mutation, optionally awaiting its result.

tasks.text: Learn: onSuccess callback — invalidate queries to refetch fresh data after mutation
tasks.description: Invalidating queries after a mutation keeps your UI in sync with the server without manual refetch logic.

tasks.text: Build: add product form with useMutation — POST and then invalidate ['products']
tasks.description: An add-product form with useMutation is the pattern you'll use for every create operation in your app.

tasks.text: Build: delete button on ProductCard — useMutation DELETE, invalidate on success
tasks.description: A delete button with cache invalidation ensures the UI updates immediately after a successful delete.

days.resource: Key pattern: After any mutation that changes the list, call queryClient.invalidateQueries(['products']) in onSuccess. This triggers a background refetch so the UI stays in sync.

tasks.text: Learn: staleTime — how long cached data is considered fresh (default 0ms)
tasks.description: staleTime controls how often TanStack Query refetches — tuning it avoids unnecessary network requests.

tasks.text: Learn: enabled option — only run query when a condition is true (e.g. user is logged in)
tasks.description: The enabled option lets you delay a query until its prerequisites (like an ID or auth token) are ready.

tasks.text: Learn: keepPreviousData (placeholderData) for smooth pagination — no flicker
tasks.description: keepPreviousData prevents the UI from flashing to a loading state when paginating.

tasks.text: Build: paginated product list — page state drives queryKey, prev/next buttons
tasks.description: A paginated product list with TanStack Query is the standard pattern for browsing large datasets.

tasks.text: Set staleTime: 1000 * 60 * 5 on product queries — avoid unnecessary refetches
tasks.description: Setting a sensible staleTime for product data avoids refetching data that rarely changes.

days.label: Day 5 · Friday
days.title: Custom hooks — organise your query logic

tasks.text: Learn: a custom hook is just a function that starts with "use" and calls other hooks
tasks.description: Custom hooks let you extract and reuse stateful logic across components — the React way to avoid duplication.

tasks.text: Build: useProducts() custom hook — encapsulates useQuery for the products list
tasks.description: useProducts() hides the query details so components just call a hook and get data.

tasks.text: Build: useProduct(id) custom hook — single product query
tasks.description: useProduct(id) follows the same pattern for a single resource — consistent and reusable.

tasks.text: Build: useCreateProduct() and useDeleteProduct() mutation hooks
tasks.description: Mutation hooks bundle the mutation and its cache invalidation logic into one reusable unit.

tasks.text: Refactor all pages to use these custom hooks — components become much cleaner
tasks.description: Refactoring to custom hooks is how production React codebases stay clean as they grow.

days.resource: Why it matters: Custom hooks are how senior React devs organise code. Your components should only worry about rendering — not about how data is fetched or cached.

weeks.askPrompt: Show me how to build custom React hooks for TanStack Query — useProducts, useProduct(id), useCreateProduct — with complete working code

weeks.title: Week 4 — Global state + auth context
weeks.description: Share data across components without prop drilling, and build a real auth system.

days.label: Day 1 · Monday
days.title: The prop drilling problem + useContext

tasks.text: Learn: prop drilling — passing props through many layers just to reach a deep child
tasks.description: Prop drilling makes code hard to maintain — recognizing it is the first step to knowing when Context helps.

tasks.text: Learn: React Context — a way to share data globally without passing props manually
tasks.description: Context lets deeply nested components access shared data without passing it through every layer.

tasks.text: Learn: createContext(), Context.Provider, useContext() hook
tasks.description: These three pieces are all you need to create and consume a Context — the full API is small.

tasks.text: Build: a ThemeContext — dark/light mode toggle accessible anywhere in the app
tasks.description: A theme toggle is a simple, classic use case that makes Context concrete before tackling auth.

tasks.text: Apply theme class to root div based on context value — Tailwind dark: classes
tasks.description: Applying a theme class based on context shows Context driving real UI changes across the app.

days.resource: Rule: Use Context for truly global state — auth user, theme, language. For server data (products, categories), keep using TanStack Query — don't put API responses in Context.

days.label: Day 2 · Tuesday
days.title: Auth context — current user + JWT storage

tasks.text: Build: AuthContext — holds user object, token, login(), logout() functions
tasks.description: AuthContext is the central place every component will check to know who's logged in.

tasks.text: Learn: store JWT in memory (useState) — safer than localStorage for auth tokens
tasks.description: Keeping the token in memory avoids exposing it to XSS attacks that can read localStorage.

tasks.text: Learn: on app load, check localStorage for persisted user info to restore session
tasks.description: Restoring session info on load means users stay logged in across page refreshes.

tasks.text: Build: login() — call the auth API (mock for now), store token, set user in context
tasks.description: login() is the function every login form will call — centralizing it keeps auth logic consistent.

tasks.text: Build: logout() — clear token and user from state and localStorage
tasks.description: logout() must clear all traces of the session so a logged-out user can't access protected data.

tasks.text: On app load, call GET /auth/me with the restored token to verify the session is still valid server-side — logout() if it fails
tasks.description: Verifying the session with /auth/me catches expired or invalid tokens that localStorage alone can't detect.

tasks.text: Update Navbar — show "Login" when logged out, "Hi Manoj · Logout" when logged in
tasks.description: A Navbar that reflects auth state is the most visible sign that your auth system is working.

tasks.text: Build: ProtectedRoute component — reads auth from context
tasks.description: ProtectedRoute is the gatekeeper that decides whether a route's content should render at all.

tasks.text: If not logged in: redirect to /login using Navigate component
tasks.description: Redirecting unauthenticated users to /login enforces access control at the routing level.

tasks.text: If logged in: render the protected child component via Outlet
tasks.description: Outlet lets ProtectedRoute wrap any number of nested routes with a single auth check.

tasks.text: Wrap admin-only routes (add product, manage orders) inside ProtectedRoute
tasks.description: Wrapping admin routes in ProtectedRoute is how you restrict the admin area to logged-in users.

tasks.text: Test: try navigating to /admin/products — confirm redirect to /login
tasks.description: Testing the redirect confirms unauthorized users genuinely can't reach protected pages.

days.label: Day 4 · Thursday
days.title: Cart state with useReducer

tasks.text: Learn: useReducer — for complex state with multiple actions (better than many useStates)
tasks.description: useReducer is better than multiple useStates when state changes involve multiple related actions.

tasks.text: Learn: dispatch({ type: 'ADD_ITEM', payload: product })
tasks.description: Dispatching typed actions makes state changes predictable and easier to debug than scattered setState calls.

tasks.text: Build: CartContext with useReducer — actions: ADD_ITEM, REMOVE_ITEM, UPDATE_QTY, CLEAR_CART
tasks.description: CartContext with useReducer is the standard pattern for shopping cart state in React e-commerce apps.

tasks.text: Build: CartPage — list cart items, show total price, remove button per item
tasks.description: CartPage is where users review and manage what they're about to buy — a core e-commerce page.

tasks.text: Show cart item count badge in Navbar from CartContext
tasks.description: A cart badge in the Navbar gives users constant visual feedback about their cart contents.

days.resource: When to use useReducer vs useState: If state has more than 2-3 related pieces that change together, or if next state depends on previous, use useReducer.

tasks.text: Build: LoginPage — email/password form, submit calls login mutation
tasks.description: LoginPage is the entry point that connects your AuthContext to a real user-facing form.

tasks.text: On login success: store token in AuthContext, navigate to /
tasks.description: Storing the token and redirecting on success completes the login flow from the user's perspective.

tasks.text: On login error: show error message from API response
tasks.description: Showing API error messages (like 'invalid credentials') gives users actionable feedback.

tasks.text: Build: RegisterPage — name, email, password, confirm password fields
tasks.description: RegisterPage is the second half of your auth flow — letting new users create accounts.

tasks.text: Client-side: confirm passwords match before submitting
tasks.description: Confirming password match client-side catches a common typo before hitting the server.

weeks.askPrompt: Show me how to build an AuthContext in React with useContext — storing the JWT token, a user object, login and logout functions, and session persistence with localStorage

weeks.title: Week 5 — UI polish + Tailwind design system
weeks.description: Make your app look professional. Design consistency, responsiveness, and reusable components.

days.label: Day 1 · Monday
days.title: Tailwind deep dive — layout and spacing

tasks.text: Learn: Flexbox in Tailwind — flex, items-center, justify-between, gap-4
tasks.description: Flexbox utilities are how you'll align and space most UI elements — navbars, cards, button groups.

tasks.text: Learn: Grid in Tailwind — grid, grid-cols-3, col-span-2
tasks.description: Grid utilities are ideal for product listings and dashboard layouts with multiple columns.

tasks.text: Learn: spacing scale — p-4 = 1rem, m-8 = 2rem, gap-6 = 1.5rem
tasks.description: Knowing the spacing scale helps you keep consistent spacing throughout your app without guesswork.

tasks.text: Learn: responsive prefixes — sm: md: lg: xl: — mobile-first approach
tasks.description: Responsive prefixes let one set of classes adapt your layout across screen sizes.

tasks.text: Redesign ProductsPage — 1 column on mobile, 2 on tablet, 3 on desktop
tasks.description: A responsive product grid is one of the most visible improvements you can make to the storefront.

tasks.text: Make Navbar collapse to hamburger menu on mobile
tasks.description: A collapsible mobile nav is expected in any modern responsive site.

days.resource: Resource: tailwindcss.com/docs — keep this open while you code. Use Ctrl+F to find any utility instantly.

days.label: Day 2 · Tuesday
days.title: Component library with shadcn/ui

tasks.text: Learn: what shadcn/ui is — copy-paste components built on Radix UI + Tailwind
tasks.description: shadcn/ui gives you accessible, customizable components without the bloat of a full UI library dependency.

tasks.text: Initialize: npx shadcn@latest init in your project
tasks.description: Initializing shadcn sets up the configuration and utilities the components depend on.

tasks.text: Add components: npx shadcn@latest add button card input badge dialog
tasks.description: Adding components generates the source code directly into your project, ready to customize.

tasks.text: Replace your custom Button/Card with shadcn versions — inspect the generated code
tasks.description: Swapping in shadcn components upgrades your UI's accessibility and polish with minimal effort.

tasks.text: Build: a product detail modal using the Dialog component
tasks.description: A modal dialog is a common UI pattern for showing details without navigating away.

days.resource: Resource: ui.shadcn.com — click any component to see the install command and copy-paste code

tasks.text: Learn: skeleton loading — show placeholder shapes while data loads (not a spinner)
tasks.description: Skeleton loaders feel faster to users than spinners because they hint at the content's shape.

tasks.text: Add: npx shadcn@latest add skeleton
tasks.description: The skeleton component gives you a ready-made building block for loading placeholders.

tasks.text: Build: ProductCardSkeleton — mirrors shape of ProductCard, shows during isLoading
tasks.description: A skeleton matching ProductCard's shape makes the loading state feel seamless.

tasks.text: Build: an ErrorFallback component for when queries fail — with a retry button
tasks.description: An ErrorFallback with retry gives users a way to recover from failed requests without reloading the page.

tasks.text: Test: throttle network in DevTools to Slow 3G — confirm skeletons appear
tasks.description: Throttling the network is the only reliable way to actually see loading states during development.

days.label: Day 4 · Thursday
days.title: Toast notifications + form with React Hook Form

tasks.text: Add: npx shadcn@latest add toast sonner
tasks.description: Toast notifications are the standard way to give non-intrusive feedback after an action.

tasks.text: Show success toast after adding product, error toast on API failure
tasks.description: Success/error toasts confirm to users whether their action (like adding a product) actually worked.

tasks.text: Install: npm install react-hook-form
tasks.description: React Hook Form reduces re-renders and boilerplate compared to manually managing form state with useState.

tasks.text: Learn: useForm(), register(), handleSubmit(), formState.errors
tasks.description: These are the core building blocks of React Hook Form — register fields, handle submission, read errors.

tasks.text: Rewrite add-product form with React Hook Form — cleaner, less boilerplate
tasks.description: Rewriting your form with React Hook Form shows the reduction in code compared to manual state management.

tasks.text: Add validation rules inside register() — required, minLength, pattern
tasks.description: Built-in validation rules let you enforce required fields and patterns without writing custom logic.

days.resource: Resource: react-hook-form.com — "Get Started" page. It replaces all the manual onChange state management for forms.

tasks.text: Learn: React.memo — prevents re-render of child if props haven't changed
tasks.description: React.memo avoids wasted re-renders of components whose props haven't changed — useful for large lists.

tasks.text: Learn: useCallback — memoize a function reference to pass as stable prop
tasks.description: useCallback prevents a function prop from changing every render, which would otherwise defeat React.memo.

tasks.text: Learn: lazy() + Suspense — code-split pages so they load on demand
tasks.description: Code-splitting with lazy/Suspense means users only download the code for the page they're viewing.

tasks.text: Apply lazy() to all page components in your router — each becomes a separate chunk
tasks.description: Lazy-loading every page reduces your initial bundle size, improving first-load performance.

tasks.text: Run: npm run build — inspect chunk sizes in the output to confirm splitting
tasks.description: Inspecting chunk sizes after a build confirms your code-splitting is actually working.

tasks.text: Learn: useMemo — memoize an expensive computed value (e.g. filtered + sorted product list) so it only recalculates when its inputs change
tasks.description: useMemo avoids recalculating expensive derived data (like filtered/sorted lists) on every render.

weeks.askPrompt: Show me how to implement React.lazy and Suspense for code-splitting all my page components in React Router — with a loading fallback

weeks.title: Week 6 — Deploy to Vercel + project polish
weeks.description: Ship your frontend. Get it live, fast, and connected to your Phase 2 backend.

days.label: Day 1 · Monday
days.title: Environment variables + API config

tasks.text: Learn: Vite env vars must start with VITE_ to be exposed to the browser
tasks.description: Vite only exposes env vars prefixed with VITE_ to client code — a security measure to avoid leaking secrets.

tasks.text: Create .env.local — VITE_API_URL=http://localhost:8080/api/v1
tasks.description: Environment variables let you point your app at different API URLs for local dev vs production.

tasks.text: Create src/api/axios.js — Axios instance with baseURL from import.meta.env.VITE_API_URL
tasks.description: A centralized Axios instance means every API call shares the same base URL and configuration.

tasks.text: Add request interceptor — automatically attach Authorization: Bearer {token} header
tasks.description: An interceptor automatically attaches the auth token, so you don't repeat that logic in every request.

tasks.text: Add response interceptor — redirect to /login on 401 response
tasks.description: A response interceptor that redirects on 401 handles expired sessions consistently across the whole app.

tasks.text: Add .env.local to .gitignore — never commit it
tasks.description: Committing .env.local would expose your local configuration — always keep it out of version control.

days.resource: This Axios setup (interceptors) is what Phase 4 is built on — your mock data will just be swapped for real API calls to your Railway backend.

days.label: Day 2 · Tuesday
days.title: Deploy to Vercel

tasks.text: Push your React project to a GitHub repo
tasks.description: Vercel deploys directly from a GitHub repo — pushing your code is the first step to deployment.

tasks.text: Create Vercel account at vercel.com — import your GitHub repo
tasks.description: Vercel's GitHub integration automatically builds and deploys your app on every push.

tasks.text: Set environment variable in Vercel dashboard: VITE_API_URL = your Railway backend URL
tasks.description: Setting the API URL in Vercel points your deployed frontend at your live Railway backend.

tasks.text: Deploy — Vercel auto-detects Vite, builds, and gives you a live URL
tasks.description: Vercel's zero-config Vite support means your first deploy can work with almost no setup.

tasks.text: Fix: add vercel.json with rewrites for SPA routing (client-side routes return 404 without it)
tasks.description: Without this rewrite rule, refreshing on a client-side route (like /products/5) returns a 404 from the server.

tasks.text: Test live URL in browser — confirm pages load and navigation works
tasks.description: Testing the live URL confirms your deployed app actually works for real users, not just localhost.

days.resource: vercel.json fix: {"rewrites": [{"source": "/(.*)", "destination": "/"}]} — add this to project root to fix direct URL access on React Router routes.

tasks.text: Learn: semantic HTML — use button for actions, a for links, nav, main, section
tasks.description: Semantic HTML elements communicate meaning to browsers and assistive technology, improving accessibility for free.

tasks.text: Learn: alt text on all img tags — describe the image, not just "image"
tasks.description: Alt text lets screen reader users understand images they can't see.

tasks.text: Learn: aria-label for icon-only buttons — screen readers read this text
tasks.description: aria-label gives screen readers a description for buttons that only contain icons.

tasks.text: Learn: keyboard navigation — Tab through your app, every interactive element reachable?
tasks.description: Keyboard navigation is essential for users who can't use a mouse — and required for accessibility compliance.

tasks.text: Install axe DevTools Chrome extension — run it on your app, fix flagged issues
tasks.description: Automated accessibility tools catch common issues quickly before manual testing.

tasks.text: Install ESLint + Prettier — configure them if not already set up by Vite
tasks.description: ESLint and Prettier catch bugs and enforce consistent formatting automatically.

tasks.text: Run ESLint — fix any warnings (unused variables, missing keys, etc.)
tasks.description: Fixing lint warnings now prevents small bugs (like missing keys) from becoming real issues.

tasks.text: Review: each component does one job — split any that feel too large
tasks.description: Splitting large components keeps your codebase maintainable as the app grows.

tasks.text: Review: no hardcoded strings or magic numbers — use constants
tasks.description: Named constants make your code self-documenting and easier to update in one place.

tasks.text: Write README.md — project description, screenshots, setup steps, live URL
tasks.description: A polished README with screenshots is often the first impression of your project for recruiters.

tasks.text: Install Vitest + React Testing Library (npm install -D vitest @testing-library/react @testing-library/user-event jsdom) and write one test that renders ProductCard and checks its name/price
tasks.description: A basic component test gives you a safety net and demonstrates testing knowledge in your portfolio.

tasks.text: Final check: run through all pages on mobile, tablet, desktop viewports
tasks.description: Testing across viewports catches responsive layout bugs before real users encounter them.

tasks.text: Run Lighthouse audit (Chrome DevTools) — aim for 90+ on Performance and Accessibility
tasks.description: Lighthouse gives you an objective score and actionable suggestions for performance and accessibility.

tasks.text: Note the Vercel URL — in Phase 4 you'll add it to Spring Boot CORS allowed origins
tasks.description: You'll need this URL in Phase 4 to allow your frontend's origin in the backend's CORS configuration.

tasks.text: Preview Phase 4: read about how Axios interceptors attach JWT to requests
tasks.description: Previewing the interceptor pattern now means Phase 4's integration will feel familiar, not new.

tasks.text: Push clean final version to GitHub — frontend repo ready to showcase
tasks.description: A clean, final push leaves your frontend repo ready to show off or build on in Phase 4.

tasks.text: Self-assessment: without notes, build a component with useState + a useQuery hook, set up a nested route with a layout, and write an AuthContext with login/logout/persistence
tasks.description: This self-assessment confirms you can combine state, data fetching, routing, and auth — the core skills for Phase 4 integration.

weeks.askPrompt: Show me the Axios instance setup in React with request and response interceptors — attaching JWT token to every request and redirecting to login on 401

weeks.title: Phase 3 project — product storefront UI
weeks.description: A complete, live React frontend — ready to wire to your Spring Boot API in Phase 4.

projectCards.heading: What you'll build
projectCards.text: A responsive e-commerce storefront with product browsing, search, filters, a working cart, login/register pages, protected admin area, and skeleton loading — all powered by mock data (JSON) ready to swap for the real API.

projectCards.heading: Pages
projectCards.text: HomePage (hero + featured products) · ProductsPage (grid + search + filter + pagination) · ProductDetailPage (image, description, add to cart) · CartPage (items, qty controls, total) · LoginPage · RegisterPage · AdminProductsPage (protected, list + add + delete)

tasks.text: React Router with nested layout (Navbar + Footer on all pages)
tasks.description: A shared layout with Navbar/Footer via nested routes keeps every page visually consistent.

tasks.text: TanStack Query for all data fetching — custom hooks for each resource
tasks.description: Custom TanStack Query hooks centralize all your data-fetching logic for easy reuse and maintenance.

tasks.text: AuthContext — login/logout/persist session, verified with /auth/me on load
tasks.description: AuthContext with session verification is what makes login state persist and stay valid across reloads.

tasks.text: CartContext with useReducer — add, remove, update quantity
tasks.description: useReducer-based cart state handles the multiple related actions (add/remove/update) a cart needs.

tasks.text: Protected route — /admin redirects to /login if not authenticated
tasks.description: A protected admin route demonstrates real access control in your frontend.

tasks.text: Skeleton loading on all data-fetching pages
tasks.description: Skeleton loading across the app gives it a polished, production feel while data loads.

tasks.text: shadcn/ui components — Button, Card, Dialog, Badge, Toast
tasks.description: shadcn/ui components give your storefront a consistent, accessible design system.

tasks.text: Fully responsive — mobile, tablet, desktop with Tailwind
tasks.description: Full responsiveness ensures the storefront works well on any device — a baseline expectation for any live site.

tasks.text: React Hook Form on all forms
tasks.description: React Hook Form on every form keeps validation and submission handling consistent throughout the app.

tasks.text: Lazy-loaded pages with Suspense fallback
tasks.description: Lazy-loaded pages keep the initial bundle small as your app grows.

tasks.text: Live on Vercel with correct SPA routing
tasks.description: A live, correctly-routed deployment is what makes this project demonstrable to others.

tasks.text: Axios instance with interceptors ready for Phase 4
tasks.description: The Axios interceptor setup is the bridge that Phase 4 will use to connect to your real backend.

tasks.text: At least one component test with Vitest + React Testing Library
tasks.description: At least one test shows you can verify component behavior, not just write it.

weeks.tip: A live, responsive React storefront with auth, cart, and admin — deployed on Vercel — is a serious portfolio piece. In Phase 4 you swap the mock data for your real Spring Boot API and the whole thing becomes one complete app.
weeks.askPrompt: Help me start building the Phase 3 project — a React e-commerce storefront. Start with the folder structure, React Router setup with nested layout, and the first two pages.
